Costa Rica Histórica · Sección

El Metate y la Mano

Tradiciones del Pacífico y del Atlántico Comparadas

The stone grinding platform — the metate — worked together with its hand stone, the mano, is not a Costa Rican invention, nor even a Central American one. It is one of the most widely distributed tools in the pre-Columbian Americas, documented from the arid highlands of central Mexico down through Guatemala, Honduras, and the rest of the Central American isthmus, with related grinding-stone technologies extending into South America as well. Mesoamerica proper — the cultural zone usually defined as central and southern Mexico, Belize, Guatemala, El Salvador, and only the border fringes of western Nicaragua and northwestern Costa Rica — sits at one end of that distribution. Guanacaste's Greater Nicoya zone is the part of Costa Rica closest to that Mesoamerican sphere, but the country as a whole, and certainly its Atlantic slope, lies outside it.

What is genuinely local is not the tool but a regional treatment of it. Across most of the Maya area, metates remained plain and strictly utilitarian, with little stylistic change over centuries. In lower Central America — Costa Rica and neighboring Panama foremost among the regions usually cited — metate carvers instead developed elaborate effigy and tripod forms: slabs raised on three or four carved legs, sometimes modeled with feline or avian heads. That stylistic elaboration, not the grinding stone itself, is the piece of material culture Costa Rica can fairly claim as a distinctive regional contribution.

This section sets two photographic traditions side by side within Costa Rica itself: a Pacific-slope postcard showing the carved tripod-metate kitchen tradition, and an Atlantic-slope photograph of Talamanca girls grinding grain on unworked river boulders — no carving at all.

Material Culture Metate & Mano Chorotega / Greater Nicoya Talamanca / Chibchan Pacific & Atlantic Slopes Postcard
Plate A

A Pueblo Woman Making TortillasMujer de Pueblo Haciendo Tortillas

Caption
“Mujer de pueblo haciendo tortillas” — printed postcard caption
Publisher
Fot. Paysser Bros., San José, C.R.
Format
Photographic postcard, gelatin silver process, sepia tone
Region
Pacific slope, Costa Rica (publisher based San José)
Cultural context
Worked tripod/footed metate; mano; wooden press; ceramic vessel on brazier
Subject
Seated woman shaping masa by hand beside grinding stone and prepared dough

The footed metate visible at right — raised on a slab base with cut-out legs — reflects the tripod/effigy elaboration associated with Greater Nicoya carving, a regional style applied to a tool used across most of Mesoamerica and the wider isthmus, not a Costa Rican original. Elsewhere in the region, metates remained largely plain and unornamented for centuries; the carved tripod form is the local stylistic contribution, not the grinding stone itself.

By the early twentieth century, when this card was issued, that earlier ceremonial elaboration had long receded into ordinary kitchen use — the tripod form persisting as plain household equipment rather than ritual or burial goods. The basins, wooden press, and covered dough on the cloth at left show the full sequence of masa preparation captured in a single frame: grinding, shaping, resting, pressing.

Plate B

Talamanca Indian Girls Grinding GrainNiñas Talamanqueñas Moliendo Grano

Caption
“Talamanca Indian Girls Grinding Grain”
Source
Costa Rica, Land of the Banana, p. 211
Photographer
H. N. Rudd
Region
Talamanca, Atlantic slope, Costa Rica
Cultural context
Unworked boulder mortars; Chibchan-derived dialect per original caption
Subject
Two girls grinding grain on natural stone, forest clearing with drying racks

Unlike the carved tripod metate of the Pacific slope, the grinding surfaces here are unmodified river boulders pressed directly into service — no quarrying, no shaping, no legs. This reflects the distinct material culture of the Atlantic slope's Chibchan-speaking peoples (Bribri and Cabécar groups historically grouped under the colonial label “Talamanca Indians”), who lie outside even the loose Mesoamerican-adjacent fringe that includes northwestern Costa Rica, and who never adopted the carved sculptural tradition associated with Greater Nicoya.

The original 1922 publication caption notes the population's relative isolation from the railroad economy and identifies the spoken dialect as derived from Guétar, the dominant tongue among local Caribbean-coast groups — terminology now superseded by modern Chibchan linguistic classification (Bribri/Cabécar), but retained here as a faithful record of the original source description. H. N. Rudd's photography is independently traceable to National Geographic, Vol. XLI No. 2 (February 1922).

Read together, these two plates show one country straddling a much larger boundary: a Mesoamerican-adjacent grinding-stone style on one coast, and an unrelated, uncarved tradition on the other — both drawing on a stone-grinding technology common to most of the hemisphere, neither one a Costa Rican invention.
